ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for pandan bread specifically; mapped to broader bread food product class. Pandan flavouring and kaya/peanut butter toppings are not represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pandan bread with kaya and peanut butter; A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible broader category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite Singaporean bread dish.

serving.description: 2 slices of pandan bread with kaya and peanut butter
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is two slices of pandan bread (approximately 80 g) each spread with kaya and peanut butter, as commonly served at Singaporean kopitiams and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Pandan Bread with Kaya and Peanut Butter — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pandan bread with kaya and peanut butter (cr_id F05020076). All per-100g values are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB entry are set to -1 (data not available).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pandan bread with kaya and peanut butter. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on white bread analogous entry (GI ~70–75) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Pandan bread uses a refined wheat flour base similar to white bread; kaya adds simple sugars which maintain a high GI. Peanut butter's fat and protein content may marginally lower the composite GI, but the dominant refined-flour and sugar components sustain a high GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 41.53 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 23.3 for an 80 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 294 mg/100 g with partial offset from dietary fibre (3.63 g/100 g) and potassium (110 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 294 mg/100 g is in the borderline-high range for hypertension; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one slice, choose unsalted peanut butter, and minimise kaya quantity to keep total sodium and sugar intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load (41.53 g/100 g) with sugar 9.55 g/100 g from refined wheat flour base and kaya
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined wheat flour base and kaya sugar produce a high glycaemic load (GL ≈ 23 per 80 g serving); individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute wholemeal pandan bread, reduce kaya quantity, use unsweetened peanut butter, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable or additional protein source to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 11.63 g/100 g with cholesterol 16.69 mg/100 g; kaya contributes coconut-derived saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Total fat is moderate at 11.63 g/100 g and peanut butter contributes heart-beneficial monounsaturated fats; however, kaya contains coconut fat with saturated fatty acids, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit kaya quantity, choose natural unsalted peanut butter, and keep serving size to one slice to manage saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Peanut butter provides MUFA-dominant fat and dietary fibre (3.63 g/100 g); saturated fat estimated below 5 g/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Peanut butter contributes monounsaturated fats, vitamin E, and polyphenols that are broadly anti-inflammatory; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should use natural unsalted peanut butter, limit kaya (which contains pro-inflammatory coconut fat), and verify peanut butter labels are free of hydrogenated oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pandan bread with kaya and peanut butter is a popular Singaporean breakfast and snack dish consisting of sliced pandan-flavoured bread spread generously with kaya (a sweet coconut and egg jam) and creamy peanut butter. Pandan bread is made from refined wheat flour enriched with pandan leaf extract or flavouring, giving it a distinctive pale green colour and a mildly sweet, fragrant aroma. Kaya is a traditional Singaporean and Malaysian jam made from coconut milk, eggs, and sugar, often flavoured with pandan, and contributes sweetness, richness, and a custard-like texture. Peanut butter adds a nutty, savoury-sweet contrast and contributes protein and monounsaturated fats. The combination is a staple at kopitiams (traditional coffee shops) and hawker centres across Singapore. Per 100 g, the dish provides 322 kcal, 12.95 g protein, 11.63 g fat, 41.53 g carbohydrate, 9.55 g sugar, 3.63 g dietary fibre, and 294 mg sodium. The high carbohydrate content and refined flour base result in a high glycaemic index (estimated ~70), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dietary fibre from peanut butter and the protein content partially moderate the glycaemic response. Calcium is notably high at 248 mg/100 g, likely from fortified bread. This dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten, eggs, and peanuts as major allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020076) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical provenance. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source; this is clearly documented.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Singaporean bread dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (F05020076) as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread (GI ~70) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for pandan bread with kaya and peanut butter was found in approved sources. Calcium value of 248 mg/100 g is notably high and likely reflects fortified bread; this is preserved from the HPB source record. The dietary_tags 'plant_based' from the input has been reviewed: kaya contains eggs, so the dish is vegetarian but not vegan or strictly plant-based; is_vegan is set to false accordingly.
